w:r.w:t: English Learning App for Japanese Speakers

Eigo Master (英語マスター) is a mobile-first English learning application built exclusively for Japanese native speakers. Unlike general-purpose language apps, every feature — explanations, hints, error feedback, and UI labels — is delivered in Japanese, eliminating the language barrier that causes beginners to abandon learning early.

To guide Japanese speakers from zero English to confident, natural communication through a structured, culturally aware curriculum — combining spaced repetition, AI-powered conversation practice, pronunciation coaching tuned to Japanese phonetic challenges, and content grounded in real-life situations relevant to Japanese learners.
Target: From 「英語が全くわからない」to conversational fluency within 12 months of consistent daily practice.
Japanese speakers face a uniquely difficult path to English fluency, driven by structural factors that generic apps ignore:
Script gap: Japanese uses three scripts (Hiragana, Katakana, Kanji) — the Roman alphabet requires explicit training
Sound system mismatch: Japanese has ~100 phonemes vs English's ~44. Sounds like L/R, V/B, TH, and consonant clusters do not exist in Japanese, causing persistent pronunciation errors
Grammar inversion: Japanese is SOV (Subject-Object-Verb); English is SVO. Sentence construction requires mental rewiring
Katakana interference: Loan words absorbed into Japanese as カタカナ (e.g., マクドナルド for McDonald's) create false pronunciation confidence
Cultural hesitancy: Japanese communication norms favor silence and indirectness — spoken practice causes anxiety in learners
Eigo Master addresses every one of these barriers by design.
Read & write Roman alphabet; 500 core words
Can read but cannot speak fluently
Web — Next.js 14 (Progressive Web App for desktop study sessions)
ALL menus, instructions, error messages, hints, and explanations are written in Japanese (日本語).
English appears ONLY as the content being learned — never as UI chrome.
Grammar explanations contrast English and Japanese structures side-by-side.
Level labels use Japanese names: 初心者, 初級, 中級, 上級.
The curriculum is structured around the CEFR (Common European Framework of Reference) mapped to familiar Japanese equivalents, and aligned with TOEIC/TOEFL band targets:

Content is organized into a branching skill tree with prerequisite gating. The tree has four tracks running in parallel — Vocabulary, Grammar, Speaking, and Listening — with cross-track dependencies (e.g., completing 'Greetings Vocabulary' unlocks 'Greetings Speaking Practice').
Each level contains 10 Units; each Unit contains 6–8 Lessons
Each Lesson: 5–12 exercises, estimated 8–15 minutes to complete
Checkpoint tests every 2 units (requires 75% to advance)
Lessons can be replayed; best score is recorded
In addition to standard English content, Eigo Master includes modules targeting Japanese-speaker pain points:
No familiarity with the Latin alphabet
Letter names, uppercase/lowercase, phonics, typing practice
Minimal pair drills, mouth-position diagrams (in Japanese), shadowing
Voiced (this) vs unvoiced (think) distinction; tongue placement exercises
Japanese uses バ for both V and B sounds
Progressive cluster drills: st, str, spl, etc.
Side-by-side comparison: Japanese vs actual English pronunciation
Rule-based explanation in Japanese + 200 targeted exercises
Japanese tense is minimal and contextual
Visual timeline diagrams; 12 English tenses with Japanese contrastive notes
4.1 Vocabulary & Flashcards (単語・フラッシュカード)
Eigo Master uses a modified SM-2 Spaced Repetition System (SRS) to schedule vocabulary reviews at the optimal moment before forgetting.
Each card tracks: ease factor (EF), interval (days), review count
• 「もう一度」(Again, rating 1): reset to 1 day, EF −0.2
EF clamped to [1.3, 2.5]. Daily new card limit: 20 (configurable)
Daily review limit: 100 (configurable). Morning push notification when reviews are due.
Front: English word + phonetic transcription (IPA) + audio button
Back: Japanese translation + example sentence (English + Japanese) + part of speech
Kanji readings shown for Japanese translations where applicable
Cards organized into themed decks: 旅行 (Travel), 仕事 (Business), 日常会話 (Daily Life), TOEIC, etc.
3,000 pre-built cards at launch; 6,000 target by v1.5
User can add custom cards from any lesson or dictionary lookup
Every grammar lesson is written entirely in Japanese with clear contrastive explanations that explicitly highlight the Japanese-to-English structural difference.
概念説明 (Concept Explanation) — Japanese explanation of the grammar rule with Japanese example sentence for context
英語構造 (English Structure) — visual sentence diagram comparing English word order to Japanese
例文 (Examples) — 5–8 examples with Japanese translation, audio, and breakdown
練習問題 (Exercises) — mixed exercise types (see below)
よくあるミス (Common Mistakes) — explicit list of errors Japanese speakers make, with corrections
並べ替え (Scrambled Words) — drag-and-drop word order reconstruction
穴埋め (Fill in the Blank) — keyboard or word-bank input
選択問題 (Multiple Choice) — select the correct English form
翻訳 (Translation) — Japanese → English free-text input (AI-graded)
誤文修正 (Error Correction) — identify and fix the mistake in a sentence
4.3 Speaking & Pronunciation (スピーキング・発音)
Speaking practice is the most anxiety-inducing skill for Japanese learners. Eigo Master reduces friction with private recording, immediate AI feedback in Japanese, and gamified repetition drills.
Learner records audio via device microphone
Audio sent to Pronunciation Service (Python / FastAPI)
Analysis pipeline: noise reduction → phoneme segmentation (wav2vec2, Japanese-English cross-lingual model) → phoneme comparison against native reference
Scores returned: Overall (0–100), Phoneme Accuracy, Rhythm & Stress, Intonation
Error report shown in Japanese: specific phonemes missed, visual mouth-position guide
Pitch accent pattern visualized as waveform overlay (native vs learner)
Learner listens to a native speaker sentence, then speaks immediately after
Three speeds: ゆっくり (slow 70%), 普通 (normal 100%), 速い (fast 130%)
After 3 rounds, scoring unlocked — learner must beat target score to advance
Focused record + AI highlight of dropped phoneme

Listening is the fastest path to comprehension improvement. All listening content is produced by native English speakers across American, British, and Australian accents — with progressive difficulty.
単語リスニング (Word Listening) — hear a word, select from 4 Japanese translations
文リスニング (Sentence Listening) — hear a sentence, type or select the English text
会話理解 (Dialogue Comprehension) — short realistic dialogues (30–90 sec) with 3–5 comprehension questions in Japanese
穴埋めディクテーション (Dictation Fill-in) — listen and type the missing word(s) in a transcript
速度トレーニング (Speed Training) — same passage at 3 speeds; goal is to understand at 130%
American English — primary accent for all vocabulary and grammar content
British English — introduced at Intermediate level; 30% of advanced listening content
Australian English — Advanced level only; 1 dedicated unit on accent recognition
All audio produced by contracted native speakers (SAG-AFTRA equivalent quality standards)
• Japanese subtitles toggle (初心者・初級 default ON, 中級 default OFF)
• Auto-pause after each sentence mode for dictation practice
Graded reading passages aligned to CEFR level (A1–B2)
Topics chosen for relevance to Japanese learners: travel, workplace emails, menus, signs, social media, news headlines
Tap-to-translate: tap any English word to see Japanese definition + part of speech
Comprehension questions in Japanese after each passage
Vocabulary encountered in reading automatically added to SRS review queue
Free-text English composition graded by AI (grammar, vocabulary, coherence)
Feedback delivered in Japanese: corrections highlighted, explanations given
Structured writing prompts at each level (e.g., Beginner: 'Write 3 sentences about yourself'; Advanced: 'Write a 200-word business email')
Keyboard assistance: built-in Roman alphabet keyboard for Beginner learners unfamiliar with mobile English input
「英文日記」(English Diary) feature: daily optional writing practice with AI feedback
4.6 AI Conversation Practice (AIとの会話練習)

The AI conversation partner allows learners to practice speaking and typing English in realistic scenarios — privately, without fear of embarrassment — with a tutor that responds in a supportive, Japanese-friendly style.
Backend: GPT-4o with structured system prompt injection
System prompt includes: learner level, known vocabulary list, scenario context, instruction to respond in English at appropriate level, and to provide Japanese corrections after each exchange
Learner input: text (any level) or voice (Intermediate+)
AI response: English reply + optional Japanese translation toggle + grammar note if an error was detected
空港でのチェックイン
Checking in a bag, asking about gate
Asking for sizes, making a return
Session transcript shown in full (English + Japanese translation)
AI highlights: grammar errors (赤), vocabulary improvements (青), natural phrasing suggestions (緑)
Score: Fluency, Accuracy, Vocabulary Range, Task Completion (1–5 each)
New vocabulary from conversation added to SRS deck automatically
Mobile: React Native (Expo SDK 51) — iOS & Android from single codebase
Web: Next.js 14 (App Router) + TypeScript — Progressive Web App
Backend API: Node.js 20 + Express — REST + WebSocket
AI Services: Python 3.12 + FastAPI — pronunciation scoring, conversation grading
AI Models: OpenAI GPT-4o (conversation), custom wav2vec2 fine-tune (pronunciation)
Infrastructure: AWS — ECS Fargate, RDS, ElastiCache, S3, CloudFront, Cognito
CI/CD: GitHub Actions → ECR → ECS rolling deploy
Navigation: React Navigation v6 — bottom tab (5 tabs) + nested stack
State management: Zustand (global: auth, settings, streak) + React Query (server state, caching)
Offline storage: WatermelonDB (SQLite) — lessons, SRS cards, cached audio
Offline sync: background queue flushes review results and XP on reconnect
Audio: Expo AV for playback; expo-speech + device microphone for recording
Animations: React Native Reanimated 3 — flashcard flip, lesson completion, streak animations
Canvas / Drawing: react-native-skia for pronunciation waveform visualization
Server Components for lesson content pages; Client Components for interactive exercises
PWA: service worker caches lesson content and vocabulary for offline use
Audio: Web Audio API for waveform visualization; MediaRecorder for pronunciation capture
Styling: Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui component primitives
Registration, login, JWT + refresh tokens, OAuth2 (Google, Apple, LINE)
Lesson data, vocabulary, grammar rules served from MongoDB with Redis cache
XP, level, streak, SRS card states — PostgreSQL
Calculates due cards, sends push notification triggers
GPT-4o system prompt injection, transcript storage, session grading
Audio analysis: wav2vec2 phoneme comparison, pitch extraction, scoring
FCM (Android), APNs (iOS), Web Push — streak reminders, review alerts
Event ingestion, learning metric aggregation, A/B test assignment
id, email, display_name, created_at, current_level, streak_count, timezone, ui_language (always 'ja')
id, english_word, ipa_phonetic, japanese_translation, audio_url, cefr_level, frequency_rank, part_of_speech, example_en, example_ja
id, user_id, item_id, item_type, due_date, interval_days, ease_factor, review_count, last_reviewed_at
id, unit_id, title_ja, level, track (vocab/grammar/speaking/listening), exercise_count, xp_reward
id, user_id, lesson_id, status, score_pct, xp_earned, completed_at, attempt_count
id, user_id, scenario_id, transcript_json, fluency_score, accuracy_score, vocab_score, task_score, duration_secs
id, user_id, target_text, audio_s3_key, overall_score, phoneme_score, rhythm_score, intonation_score, errors_json
id, user_id, amount, source_type, source_id, earned_at
limit, deck_type → card list with front/back/audio
日本語ファースト (Japanese-First): Every UI label, tooltip, error, and guide is in Japanese
低不安設計 (Low-Anxiety Design): Mistakes are framed as learning opportunities (ナイスチャレンジ！), not failures
Minimal English in chrome: Learners should not need to read English to navigate the app
Clarity over density: Clean layouts with generous whitespace — avoid overwhelming beginners
Consistency: Uniform exercise patterns so learners focus on content, not interface
Daily streak, XP bar, due SRS count, today's recommended lesson, motivational message in Japanese
Visual lesson tree; locked/unlocked/completed states; level sections separated by checkpoint gates
Progress bar, exercise type label in Japanese, answer input, hint button (Japanese hint), XP earned on completion
Card flip animation; 4-button rating (もう一度 / 難しい / 普通 / 簡単); session summary
Waveform display; record button; playback; score breakdown in Japanese; retry option
Chat bubble UI; Japanese translation toggle; post-session review panel
Audio player with speed control; transcript (toggleable); answer input below
Streak calendar, XP timeline, level progress, vocabulary mastered count, weak areas radar chart
Daily goal, notification time, card limits, account management — all labels in Japanese
WCAG 2.1 AA compliance — minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio for all text
Full VoiceOver (iOS) and TalkBack (Android) support with Japanese accessibility labels
Minimum touch target: 44×44pt for all interactive elements
Dynamic Type: respects system font size settings (iOS) and font scale (Android)
All audio content has Japanese text transcripts
Color-blind safe palette: error/success states use both color and icon shape
毎日の目標 (Daily Goal): configurable — 5 / 10 / 15 / 20 minutes or custom
ストリークシールド (Streak Shield): earned at 7, 30, 90-day milestones; protects streak for 1 missed day (max 2 stored)
リマインダー (Reminders): smart push notification timing based on user's historically active hour
月次チャレンジ (Monthly Challenge): e.g., '今月100単語覚えよう！', '会話練習を5回しよう！'
友達ランキング (Friend Leaderboard): opt-in weekly XP leaderboard among friends (LINE integration for sharing)
Core vocabulary: Oxford 3000 + Academic Word List, filtered and organized by CEFR level
TOEIC vocabulary: Official TOEIC Institute word lists (N500, N1000, N1500 frequency tiers)
Japanese translations: EDICT/JMdict (open-license Japanese-English dictionary) + editorial review by certified Japanese-English translators
Grammar explanations: 100% original authored content by CELTA/DELTA-certified teachers with Japanese-speaker specialization
Example sentences: Original, curated — no machine-translated content in learner-facing material
All vocabulary and grammar audio recorded by contracted native speakers (American English primary accent)
British and Australian accent content recorded by separate native speaker contractors
Listening dialogue scripts written by native speakers and reviewed for naturalness and cultural accuracy
Audio format: MP3 (192kbps) + OGG fallback; stored on AWS S3, delivered via CloudFront CDN
Target library: 6,000 vocabulary audio files + 500 dialogue segments at launch
CMS: Strapi (self-hosted) — role-based access for Admins, Content Editors, Language Reviewers, QA
Workflow: Draft → Review → Approved → Published; full change history
Lesson builder: WYSIWYG editor for exercise creation with real-time preview
Bulk import: vocabulary via CSV upload with automated validation pipeline
Content export: Strapi webhooks trigger MongoDB update + Redis cache invalidation on publish
Japanese learners often study during commutes (電車の中) — offline capability for core features is essential.
Cards cached locally; results synced on reconnect via queue
User initiates download per unit; stored in WatermelonDB
Auto-download audio for next 2 units when on Wi-Fi
Requires API call; banner shown with grammar exercise fallback
Requires server-side model; local recording saved for sync later
XP and progress queued locally; bulk sync on reconnect
Requires live data; cached last-known state displayed
Sync conflict resolution: last-write-wins for progress; server is authoritative for content versions
Offline indicator shown in top-right of screen with estimated sync queue count
Background fetch (iOS) / WorkManager (Android) attempt sync every 15 minutes when app is backgrounded
Access to Beginner level (初心者) — all units
Elementary level: first 2 units only (paywall at unit 3)
10.2 Eigo Master Plus (プレミアムプラン)
Full access to all 4 levels and all tracks
Unlimited SRS reviews; configurable daily limits
Full pronunciation scoring with phoneme-level feedback
iOS: Apple StoreKit 2 — in-app purchase with server-side receipt validation
Android: Google Play Billing Library 6 — purchase token server validation
Web: Stripe — recurring subscription with Japanese locale support (¥ currency)
LINE Pay: Optional integration for Japanese users preferring LINE ecosystem payments
Receipt validation handled by Auth Service before premium flag is set on user record
Authentication: JWT (15-min expiry) + rotating refresh tokens (7-day); HttpOnly cookies (web), iOS Keychain / Android Keystore (mobile)
Transport: TLS 1.3 enforced on all endpoints; HSTS preloaded
Audio data: pronunciation recordings purged from S3 within 24 hours of scoring; opt-in to retain for model improvement
Japanese Privacy Law (個人情報保護法 — APPI) compliance: data export and deletion available in-app within 72 hours
GDPR compliance for EU residents: consent log, right to erasure, data portability
PCI DSS: no card data stored; all payment tokenized by Stripe / Apple / Google
Dependency scanning: Snyk in CI pipeline; SAST via CodeQL on all PRs
Penetration testing: quarterly by third-party security firm
APM: Datadog — service latency, error rates, API throughput
Error tracking: Sentry — frontend (RN + Next.js) + backend (Node + Python) with release tracking
Uptime: Pingdom synthetic checks on 5 critical flows (login, lesson load, SRS review, conversation start, pronunciation score)
Alerting: PagerDuty on-call; P0 (service down) < 5min response; P1 (degraded) < 30min
SLA target: 99.9% monthly uptime on all core learning APIs
SRS algorithm, scoring logic, API controllers — 80% coverage
API contracts, DB interactions, auth flows
Onboarding, lesson completion, SRS session, subscription flow
Same critical journeys on Chrome, Safari, Firefox
Automated a11y scan on all screens per release
All UI labels confirm Japanese rendering correctly
API load test: P95 < 300ms at 15,000 concurrent users
Pronunciation scoring consistency and regression tests
iOS: 15 devices (iPhone SE → iPhone 16 Pro Max); Android: 25 devices
Auth, CMS, SRS engine, Beginner level (全初心者コンテンツ), vocabulary deck (500 words), audio pipeline
Elementary level, pronunciation scoring MVP, L/R and TH drills, listening exercises
AI conversation partner (5 scenarios), post-session grading, Intermediate level content
Advanced level, business English module, TOEIC prep, gamification, offline sync
Closed beta (500 Japanese learners), accessibility audit, app store submission, marketing launch
British/Australian accent content, LINE Pay integration, friend leaderboard, v1.5 content expansion
1 × ML Engineer — wav2vec2 pronunciation model fine-tuning
1 × UX/UI Designer (Japanese UX patterns experience preferred)
2 × Content Writers / Curriculum Designers (CELTA/DELTA certified, Japanese L1)
1 × Native Speaker Audio Director + contracted voice talent
SRS (スペースド・レペティション)
Spaced Repetition System — algorithm scheduling reviews at optimal intervals for long-term memory retention
SuperMemo 2 — the foundational spaced repetition algorithm (Wozniak, 1987) that EigoMaster's SRS is based on
Common European Framework of Reference — A1 (beginner) to C2 (mastery); universal language proficiency standard
Test of English for International Communication — the most common English proficiency exam in Japan
International Phonetic Alphabet — standardized symbols for representing pronunciation (e.g., /l/, /r/, /θ/)
A speaking technique where learners speak simultaneously with or immediately after a native speaker recording
The most common English vowel sound — unstressed, central — does not exist in Japanese; critical for natural English
When English loanwords absorbed into Japanese as Katakana create false pronunciation expectations for learners
Subject-Object-Verb (Japanese) vs Subject-Verb-Object (English) — the fundamental word-order difference
Facebook AI's speech representation model used as the base for EigoMaster's pronunciation scoring engine
OpenAI's multimodal language model used to power the AI conversation partner feature
JSON Web Token — a secure, compact token format used for user authentication between client and server
Progressive Web App — web application with native-app capabilities including offline access and home screen installation
Act on Protection of Personal Information — Japan's primary data privacy law, equivalent to GDPR
